an10.60:1.1: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
an10.60:1.2: Now at that time Venerable Girimānanda was afflicted, suffering, gravely ill.
an10.60:1.3: Then Venerable Ānanda approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side. Seated to one side, Venerable Ānanda said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an10.60:10.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of cessation?
an10.60:10.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:10.3: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling [*samatho*] of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishment of all acquisitions, the ending of *taṇhā*, cessation, *nibbāna*.’
an10.60:10.4: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of cessation.
an10.60:11.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of non-delight in the whole world?
an10.60:11.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* dwells abandoning and not taking up the forms of *upādāna*, mental standpoints, adherences, and underlying tendencies in the world.
an10.60:11.3: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of non-delight in the whole world.
an10.60:12.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of non-desire toward all *saṅkhāras*?
an10.60:12.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* is troubled by all *saṅkhāras*, feels shame toward them, and is disgusted with them.
an10.60:12.3: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of non-desire toward all *saṅkhāras*.
an10.60:13.1: And what, Ānanda, is *sati* of in-breathing and out-breathing?
an10.60:13.10: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the mental *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the mental *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.11: He trains thus: ‘Calming the mental *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Calming the mental *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.12: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing mind [*citta*], I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing mind [*citta*], I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.19: He trains thus: ‘Contemplating relinquishment, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Contemplating relinquishment, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, sits down cross-legged, sets his body upright, and establishes *sati* in front of him.
an10.60:13.20: This is called, Ānanda, *sati* of in-breathing and out-breathing.
an10.60:13.3: With *sati* alone he breathes in; with *sati* alone he breathes out.
an10.60:13.4: Breathing in long, he understands, ‘I breathe in long.’ Breathing out long, he understands, ‘I breathe out long.’
an10.60:13.5: Breathing in short, he understands, ‘I breathe in short.’ Breathing out short, he understands, ‘I breathe out short.’
an10.60:13.6: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.7: He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.8: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *pīti*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *pīti*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:13.9: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *sukha*, I will breathe in.’ He trains thus: ‘Experiencing *sukha*, I will breathe out.’
an10.60:14.1: If, Ānanda, you were to speak to the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda about these ten *saññās*, it is possible that, on hearing these ten *saññās*, that affliction of the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda would immediately subside.”
an10.60:15.1: Then Venerable Ānanda learned these ten *saññās* in the presence of the *Bhagavā* and approached Venerable Girimānanda; having approached, he spoke to Venerable Girimānanda about these ten *saññās*.
an10.60:15.2: Then, when Venerable Girimānanda heard the ten *saññās*, that affliction immediately subsided.
an10.60:15.3: And Venerable Girimānanda recovered from that affliction. And that affliction of Venerable Girimānanda was thus abandoned.
an10.60:15.5: The One’s Own Mind Chapter is the first.
an10.60:2.1: “Venerable Girimānanda, *bhante*, is afflicted, suffering, gravely ill.
an10.60:2.2: It would be *sādhu*, *bhante*, if the *Bhagavā* would approach Venerable Girimānanda out of compassion.”
an10.60:2.3: “If, Ānanda, you were to speak to the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda about ten *saññās*, it is possible that, on hearing those ten *saññās*, that affliction of the *bhikkhu* Girimānanda would immediately subside.
an10.60:3.2: The *saññā* of impermanence [*aniccasaññā*], the *saññā* of not-self [*anattasaññā*], the *saññā* of the unattractive, the *saññā* of danger, the *saññā* of abandoning, the *saññā* of fading, the *saññā* of cessation, the *saññā* of non-delight in the whole world, the *saññā* of non-desire toward all *saṅkhāras*, and *sati* of in-breathing and out-breathing.
an10.60:4.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of impermanence [*aniccasaññā*]?
an10.60:4.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:4.3: ‘form [*rūpaṁ*] is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *vedanā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saññā* is impermanent [*aniccā*], *saṅkhāras* are impermanent [*aniccā*], *viññāṇa* is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*].’
an10.60:4.4: Thus he dwells contemplating impermanence [*aniccā*] in these five aggregates of *upādāna*.
an10.60:4.5: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of impermanence [*aniccasaññā*].
an10.60:5.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of not-self [*anattasaññā*]?
an10.60:5.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:5.3: ‘the eye is not-self [*anattā*], forms [*rūpā*] are not-self [*anattā*], the ear is not-self [*anattā*], sounds are not-self [*anattā*], the nose is not-self [*anattā*], odors are not-self [*anattā*], the tongue is not-self [*anattā*], tastes are not-self [*anattā*], the body is not-self [*anattā*], tangibles are not-self [*anattā*], the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*].’
an10.60:5.4: Thus he dwells contemplating not-self [*anattā*] in these six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*].
an10.60:5.5: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of not-self [*anattasaññā*].
an10.60:6.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of the unattractive?
an10.60:6.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, upward from the soles of the feet and downward from the tips of the hair, bounded by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
an10.60:6.3: ‘There are in this body head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin; flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys; heart, liver, diaphragm, spleen, lungs; intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces; bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat; tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, urine.’
an10.60:6.4: Thus he dwells contemplating the unattractive in this body.
an10.60:6.5: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of the unattractive.
an10.60:7.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of danger?
an10.60:7.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:7.3: ‘This body has much *dukkha*, much danger. Thus in this body various afflictions arise, such as—
an10.60:7.4: eye disease, ear disease, nose disease, tongue disease, body disease, head disease, outer-ear disease, mouth disease, tooth disease, lip disease, cough, asthma, catarrh, fever, old age, stomach disease, fainting, dysentery, stabbing pains, cholera, leprosy, boils, eczema, consumption, epilepsy, ringworm, itch, scabies, nail disease, scab, blood disorder, bile disorder, diabetes, hemorrhoids, pimples, fistulas, afflictions originating from bile, afflictions originating from phlegm, afflictions originating from wind, afflictions from their combination, afflictions born from seasonal change, afflictions born from uneven care, afflictions from assault, afflictions born from the result of *kamma*, cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, and urination.’
an10.60:7.5: Thus he dwells contemplating danger in this body.
an10.60:7.6: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of danger.
an10.60:8.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of abandoning?
an10.60:8.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* does not tolerate an arisen sensual thought [*kāmavitakkaṁ*]; he abandons it, dispels it, ends it, and brings it to non-existence. He does not tolerate an arisen thought [*vitakkaṁ*] of ill will; he abandons it, dispels it, ends it, and brings it to non-existence. He does not tolerate an arisen thought [*vitakkaṁ*] of harming; he abandons it, dispels it, ends it, and brings it to non-existence. He does not tolerate bad, unskillful *dhammas* whenever they arise; he abandons them, dispels them, ends them, and brings them to non-existence.
an10.60:8.3: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of abandoning.
an10.60:9.1: And what, Ānanda, is the *saññā* of fading?
an10.60:9.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
an10.60:9.3: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling [*samatho*] of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishment of all acquisitions, the ending of *taṇhā*, fading, *nibbāna*.’
an10.60:9.4: This is called, Ānanda, the *saññā* of fading.
